Sun.what: The star that provides most of the energy driving Earth systems.
Sun.why: It powers climate, ecosystems and many renewable technologies.

Solar radiation.what: Energy from the sun moving as electromagnetic waves.
Solar radiation.why: It is the starting point for solar technologies and photosynthesis.

Solar panels.what: Devices that convert sunlight into electricity.
Solar panels.why: They provide renewable electricity without direct fuel combustion.

Photovoltaics.what: The process that converts light into electrical energy in solar cells.
Photovoltaics.why: It explains how sunlight becomes electricity.

Electricity generation.what: The production of electrical power from energy sources.
Electricity generation.why: It powers modern homes, schools, hospitals and industries.

Electricity grid.what: The network that transports electricity from producers to users.
Electricity grid.why: Without grids, generated electricity cannot reach most people reliably.

Homes.what: Buildings that use electricity for lighting, cooling, appliances and digital devices.
Homes.why: Homes make energy systems visible in daily life.

Lower carbon emissions.what: Reduced release of greenhouse gases from energy use.
Lower carbon emissions.why: It helps slow climate change and improve long-term sustainability.

Wind.what: Moving air created by differences in heating and pressure.
Wind.why: It is a renewable energy source that can be converted into electricity.

Kinetic energy.why: It helps students understand how movement can become useful power.

Wind turbines.what: Machines that turn wind energy into mechanical motion.
Wind turbines.why: They connect natural airflow to electricity generation.

Generators.what: Devices that convert mechanical energy into electrical energy.
Generators.why: They are central to many power systems, from wind to hydro.

Urban electricity use.what: Electricity consumed in cities for buildings, transport and infrastructure.
Urban electricity use.why: Cities concentrate demand and reveal how critical energy planning is.

Reduced emissions.what: A decrease in harmful pollutants and greenhouse gases.
Reduced emissions.why: It is one sign that energy systems are becoming more sustainable.

Coal.what: A fossil fuel formed from ancient organic matter.
Coal.why: It has powered development but also produces major emissions when burned.

Chemical energy.why: It helps explain how fuels release energy through combustion.

Coal power plant.what: A facility that burns coal to generate electricity.
Coal power plant.why: It illustrates how fossil fuel systems connect energy supply to climate impacts.

Combustion.what: A chemical reaction that releases energy, usually as heat.
Combustion.why: It is central to many traditional energy systems and emissions.

Carbon emissions.what: The release of carbon dioxide and related greenhouse gases.
Carbon emissions.why: They are strongly linked to climate change.

Greenhouse effect.what: The trapping of heat in Earth's atmosphere by certain gases.
Greenhouse effect.why: It explains why rising emissions warm the planet.

Climate change.what: Long-term changes in climate driven strongly today by human activities.
Climate change.why: It connects energy choices to real environmental and social consequences.

Solar energy.what: Energy from sunlight that can be converted into useful forms.
Solar energy.why: It is a major renewable resource.

Battery storage.what: Stored electrical energy that can be used later.
Battery storage.why: It helps renewable systems become more reliable.

Stored electricity.what: Electricity kept for later use through storage systems.
Stored electricity.why: It allows supply and demand to be better matched.

Demand balancing.what: Managing when electricity is supplied and used.
Demand balancing.why: It helps prevent waste and improve grid performance.

Reliable supply.what: Consistent access to electricity when needed.
Reliable supply.why: It is essential for households, health systems and economies.

Grid stability.what: The ability of the grid to keep electricity flowing smoothly and safely.
Grid stability.why: Stable grids are essential when energy sources are variable.

Energy transition.what: The shift from high-carbon systems to more sustainable energy systems.
Energy transition.why: It is one of the biggest challenges and opportunities of this century.

Photosynthesis.what: The process by which plants turn sunlight into chemical energy.
Photosynthesis.why: It is how solar energy enters ecosystems.

Plant growth.what: The development of plants using energy and materials from the environment.
Plant growth.why: It stores energy that supports wider food systems.

Stored chemical energy.what: Energy stored in molecules produced by living or non-living systems.
Stored chemical energy.why: It helps explain food chains and fuels.

Food chains.what: Sequences showing how energy moves through organisms.
Food chains.why: They show that energy is transferred and reduced across trophic levels.

Ecosystem energy flow.what: The movement of energy through natural systems.
Ecosystem energy flow.why: It connects ecology, sustainability and energy literacy.

Life on Earth.what: Living systems supported by energy flows and cycles.
Life on Earth.why: It emphasises the importance of energy beyond technology.

Electricity.what: A versatile form of energy that powers many technologies.
Electricity.why: It links production, infrastructure and daily use.

Battery materials.what: Materials such as lithium, nickel and graphite used in batteries.
Battery materials.why: They connect clean technology to mining, supply chains and recycling.

Electric vehicles.what: Vehicles powered fully or partly by electricity.
Electric vehicles.why: They can reduce emissions and air pollution when paired with cleaner grids.

Charging systems.what: Infrastructure that delivers electricity to electric vehicles.
Charging systems.why: They show that transport transition depends on wider systems.

Transport energy.what: Energy used to move people and goods.
Transport energy.why: Transport is a major part of modern energy demand.

Lower air pollution.what: A reduction in harmful pollutants in the air.
Lower air pollution.why: It improves public health and quality of life.

Depends on cleaner grids.what: The idea that some technologies are only truly sustainable when electricity sources also improve.
Depends on cleaner grids.why: It teaches systems thinking rather than isolated solutions.

Energy demand.what: The amount of energy needed by a system or community.
Energy demand.why: Understanding demand is essential for planning sustainable systems.

Building use.what: How buildings consume energy through lighting, cooling, equipment and occupancy.
Building use.why: It connects sustainability to places students know well.

Energy efficiency.what: Achieving the same service with less energy.
Energy efficiency.why: It is one of the fastest ways to reduce emissions and costs.

Smart monitoring.what: Using data and sensors to track and manage energy use.
Smart monitoring.why: It helps users make better decisions about energy systems.

Lower bills.why: It links sustainability to practical benefits.

Student action.what: Learner participation in identifying and improving systems.
Student action.why: It builds student agency and ownership.

Internet use.what: Digital activity that depends on connected devices and online services.
Internet use.why: It seems invisible but still requires energy.

Cloud services.what: Online computing and storage systems hosted in remote infrastructure.
Cloud services.why: They connect digital behaviour to real physical energy use.

Data centres.what: Facilities that store, process and transmit digital information.
Data centres.why: They are a major and often unseen source of electricity demand.

Cooling systems.why: They show how energy use creates further energy needs.

Digital convenience.what: Ease and speed created by digital systems.
Digital convenience.why: It is useful, but it also has an energy cost.

Large energy footprint.what: A high level of energy demand linked to a product, service or system.
Large energy footprint.why: It encourages students to look beyond what is visible.

Need for efficiency.what: The requirement to reduce wasted energy while keeping services useful.
Need for efficiency.why: It shows why sustainability often starts with using energy better.
